Q1.bottleneck: Memory bandwidth during scan and arithmetic computation in scalar inner loop
Q1.root_cause: Q1 is processing 59M rows with 6 aggregate computations per row in a scalar loop. Zone maps filter only 2% of data (98% passes shipdate filter). Direct-indexed aggregation is optimal (no hash table overhead). The remaining bottleneck is: (1) Sequential loads of 7 columns per row (quantity, price, discount, tax, returnflag_code, linestatus_code, shipdate), (2) Arithmetic operations (discount_factor, disc_price, charge) without SIMD, (3) 6 accumulations per qualifying row. Compiler autovectorization is limited by complex aggregation pattern with indexed updates.
Q1.benchmark_gap: 1.25x slower than DuckDB (249ms vs 199ms). Gap is very small - Q1 is well-optimized. DuckDB likely uses explicit SIMD for vectorized arithmetic (4 doubles at once with AVX2) and potentially prefetching. Further optimization has diminishing returns.

Q3.bottleneck: Sequential single-threaded execution of large scans and joins
Q3.root_cause: Q3 performs three sequential operations: (1) Scan 1.5M customers to build 1.5M-bit bitmap (30ms), (2) Scan 15M orders with zone map filtering (~6M qualify) to build order_info hash map with 500K entries (300ms), (3) Scan 60M lineitem rows with zone map filtering (~20M qualify) and probe order_info for each (2000ms). The lineitem scan dominates at 2000ms+. Despite Robin Hood hash maps being 2-3x faster than std::unordered_map, the sheer volume of work (20M filtered rows × hash probe + aggregation) is the bottleneck. DuckDB likely uses: (1) Partitioned hash joins to improve cache locality, (2) Parallel execution across multiple threads (4-8 cores), (3) Bloom filters to avoid probing the order_info hash table for keys that don't exist. Current implementation is single-threaded and processes all data sequentially.
Q3.benchmark_gap: 17.5x slower than DuckDB (2369ms vs 135ms). This is the CRITICAL bottleneck. Q3 accounts for 83% of total query time (2369ms out of 2639ms total). DuckDB's 135ms suggests they're using 4-8 threads in parallel plus additional optimizations (bloom filters, better join algorithms, SIMD aggregation). Our current implementation has achieved all the foundational optimizations but is fundamentally limited by single-threaded execution.

Q6.bottleneck: None - Q6 is FASTER than DuckDB!
Q6.root_cause: Q6 benefits heavily from: (1) Zone maps skipping 86% of data blocks (7 years of data, filtering to 1 year), (2) SIMD vectorization processing 8 rows at once with AVX2 for all 4 predicates, (3) Tight inner loop with minimal branching. The AVX2 SIMD implementation is working extremely well. At 21ms, Q6 is 1.3x FASTER than DuckDB's 27ms. No further optimization needed.
Q6.benchmark_gap: BEATING DuckDB by 1.3x (21ms vs 27ms). Q6 is fully optimized and demonstrates that our implementation can match or exceed DuckDB when all optimizations are applied.

analysis.overall: Iteration 2 delivered massive improvements, especially on Q6 which now beats DuckDB. Q1 is close to optimal (1.25x gap). However, Q3 remains 17.5x slower than DuckDB and accounts for 83% of total execution time. The root cause is clear: Q3 is doing 20M+ hash probes and aggregations in a single thread, while DuckDB likely uses parallel execution (4-8 threads) plus bloom filter optimizations. The next iteration MUST focus on parallelizing Q3 to close this gap. Secondary optimizations include bloom filters for Q3 semi-joins and potentially multi-threaded execution for Q1 (though the gains are smaller).

performance_optimizations.technique: Multi-threaded parallel execution with thread-local aggregation
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-4x speedup on Q3 (2369ms -> 590-790ms). With 4 threads, expected near-linear scaling on the 2000ms lineitem phase to ~500ms. Total Q3 time: ~660-830ms (vs DuckDB's 135ms target, closing gap from 17.5x to 4.9-6.1x).
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: parallelism/thread-parallelism.md - Morsel-driven parallelism with thread-local aggregation

performance_optimizations.technique: Bloom filter for order_info hash table probe
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30% additional speedup on Q3 lineitem phase. If combined with Priority 1 (parallelization), Q3 total: 660-830ms -> 520-650ms. Net improvement: 3.6-4.6x faster than current (2369ms -> 515-660ms).
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: indexing/bloom-filters.md - Bloom filters for semi-join reduction

performance_optimizations.technique: Multi-threaded parallel aggregation with morsel-driven execution
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-3.5x speedup on Q1 (249ms -> 70-85ms). This would make Q1 2.3-2.8x FASTER than DuckDB's 199ms, demonstrating our implementation can beat DuckDB when fully optimized.
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: parallelism/thread-parallelism.md - Thread-local aggregation with merge phase

performance_optimizations.technique: Prefetch hash table buckets during lineitem probe
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-15% additional speedup on Q3 (if combined with Priority 1+2: 515-660ms -> 440-560ms). Total improvement: 4.2-5.4x faster than current.
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: data-structures/compact-hash-tables.md - Prefetching for hash table probes

performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-15% speedup on Q1 (249ms -> 210-225ms if standalone, or 70-85ms -> 60-75ms if combined with Priority 3). Diminishing returns given complexity.

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace std::partial_sort with heap-based Top-K selection
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Negligible (<1%, ~5ms). std::partial_sort is already optimal for k=10. Not recommended unless Q3 is fully optimized by higher-priority techniques.

summary: Iteration 2 achieved excellent results on Q1 (1.25x from DuckDB) and Q6 (BEATING DuckDB by 1.3x), but Q3 remains the critical bottleneck at 17.5x slower than DuckDB. The root cause is clear: single-threaded execution doing 2000ms+ of hash probes and aggregations. Priority 1 optimization (multi-threaded Q3) is MANDATORY and expected to deliver 3-4x speedup, closing the gap from 17.5x to 4.9-6.1x. Priority 2 (bloom filters) adds another 20-30% on top for 4.2-5.4x total improvement. If both are implemented successfully, Q3 should reach 440-660ms (vs DuckDB's 135ms target, a 3.3-4.9x gap). Priority 3 (multi-threaded Q1) can potentially make Q1 FASTER than DuckDB. Focus iteration 3 on Priority 1 and 2 (Q3 parallelization + bloom filters) as these deliver the highest impact with medium/low risk. If time permits, add Priority 3 (Q1 parallelization) for additional gains.
